gist_memory.paper_problem_memory: The paper addresses the challenge of identifying optimal combinations of linker and aggregation-inducing tag for catalytically active inclusion bodies (CatIBs) of a given enzyme, as no a priori prediction is possible. The goal is to develop a high-throughput, semi-automated Design-Build-Test-Learn (DBTL) workflow that integrates automated strain construction, cultivation, purification, and characterization, combined with Bayesian optimization (Thompson sampling) to efficiently screen large CatIB libraries and identify the best-performing variant.

section_gists.heading: Abstract
section_gists.gist: Summarizes the development of a semi-automated cloning workflow to generate 63 CatIB variants of BsGDH, optimization of an automated screening workflow (eliminating plate position effects by changing cultivation temperature), and application of Bayesian process model with Thompson sampling to screen variants in three rounds, identifying TDoT-PT-BsGDH as the best performer. Manual workload reduced from 59 to 7 h for 48 variants (88% reduction).
process_signals: Semi-automated cloning workflow implemented for fast generation of 63 CatIB variants.
Automated CatIB screening workflow developed, optimized, and validated using BsGDH-PT-CBDCell.
Optimization steps included changing cultivation temperature to eliminate positional bias.
Bayesian process model and Thompson sampling used for iterative screening.
63 variants screened in only three batch experiments.
TDoT-PT-BsGDH identified as best CatIB performer.
metrics: Manual workload reduction: 59 h to 7 h for 48 variants (88%)
Reproducibility: 1.9% relative standard deviation across 42 biological replicates

section_gists.heading: Background
section_gists.gist: CatIBs are inclusion bodies retaining catalytic activity, formed by fusing an aggregation-inducing tag to an enzyme via a linker. Choice of linker and tag strongly influences activity, but no a priori prediction is possible, necessitating extensive screening. Previous studies used laborious traditional cloning. Golden Gate Assembly (GGA) is faster and automatable. High-throughput screening requires automation of cultivation, purification, and analytics. Bayesian optimization and Thompson sampling can efficiently plan experiments in DBTL cycles. This study aims to combine workflow automation with process modeling for CatIB screening.
process_signals: CatIB activity depends on linker, tag, and fusion terminus; no a priori prediction possible.
Traditional cloning is laborious; GGA is faster and automatable.
High-throughput screening requires automation of cultivation, purification, and analytics.
Bayesian optimization and Thompson sampling can enhance experimental design in DBTL cycles.
Goal: combine automation with process modeling for CatIB DBTL.
failures_or_limits: A priori prediction of suitable combinations not possible.
N-terminal fusion often failed in previous studies.

section_gists.heading: Semi-automated strain construction to generate 63 different BsGDH-CatIBs
section_gists.gist: A semi-automated cloning workflow using GGA was implemented to construct up to 96 CatIB variants in parallel. Transformation and GGA were fully automated using Opentrons; plasmid preparation was parallelized. Manual work reduced to 11 h for 96 variants (17% of traditional). Applied to generate 63 BsGDH-CatIB variants with SG/PT linkers, eight tags, and N-/C-terminal fusions, plus linker length and tag length studies. 13 out of 76 constructs failed (potentially non-viable), leaving 63 for screening.
process_signals: Semi-automated workflow targeted repetitive steps; transformation and GGA automated.
Manual work: 11 h for 96 variants vs. traditional cloning.
63 variants successfully constructed; 13 failed (non-viable phenotype).
Variants included linker type, tag type, fusion terminus, linker length, tag length.
metrics: Manual work: 11 h for 96 variants (17% of traditional)
failures_or_limits: 13 CatIB variants could not be cloned within three attempts, potentially due to non-viable phenotype.

section_gists.heading: Overview of the CatIB screening workflow
section_gists.gist: Automated screening workflow includes parallelized cultivation in BioLector I with FlowerPlate, followed by automated CatIB purification (centrifugation, washing, lysis, resuspension) and enzymatic assay with online NADH fluorescence measurement at 37°C. Pre-warming at 40°C for 10 min to avoid temperature effects.
process_signals: Automation of cultivation, purification, and assay essential for high-throughput.
Purification steps: centrifugation, washing, lysis with BugBuster+lysozyme, separation of soluble/insoluble fractions.
Assay: pre-warming, online NADH fluorescence measurement.

section_gists.heading: Optimization of the CatIB workflow – BioLector positional bias
section_gists.gist: Initial cultivation protocol (37°C 3h, 15°C 69h) caused systematic positional bias in BioLector backscatter values due to temperature gradient from cooling fan. 48 replicates of BsGDH-PT-CBDCell showed backscatter range 60-80 a.u. (mean 74.9, SD 4.5). Changing to constant 25°C eliminated bias (mean 70.2, SD 1.1). Microscopy and activity tests confirmed CatIB formation and activity at 25°C, with some variants showing improved formation. 25°C adopted for screening.
process_signals: Positional bias observed: lower backscatter in bottom left, higher in top right.
Hypothesis: temperature gradient from cooling fan during 15°C step.
Solution: constant 25°C cultivation eliminated bias.
Validation: CatIB formation and activity confirmed at 25°C; some variants preferred 25°C.
metrics: Backscatter SD reduced from 4.5 a.u. to 1.1 a.u.
48 biological replicates used for bias assessment
failures_or_limits: Initial protocol caused unfair ranking due to positional bias.

section_gists.heading: Optimization of the CatIB workflow – Enzymatic assay and overall validation
section_gists.gist: Enzymatic assay adapted from manual study: switched from sampling/quenching to online NADH fluorescence measurement for higher data density; added pre-warming to avoid temperature effects. Validation with 42 replicates showed high reproducibility (1.9% RSD after 120 min vs. 11.4% previously). Automated workflow reduced manual work from 40 h to 1 h for 48 variants (plus 11 h automated). Overall manual work for construction and screening: 7 h for 48 variants (12% of manual).
process_signals: Assay changed to online measurement for higher resolution.
Pre-warming added to avoid temperature effects.
Validation: 1.9% RSD across 42 replicates.
Manual work reduced by >90% for screening.
metrics: Manual work: 1 h for 48 variants (screening), 7 h total (construction + screening)

section_gists.heading: Automated screening of 63 BsGDH-CatIBs via Thompson sampling
section_gists.gist: Combined automated cloning, cultivation, and characterization with Bayesian process model and Thompson sampling to screen 63 variants in three rounds (FlowerPlate capacity 48). Process model describes assay kinetics as first-order reaction, accounting for dilution, pipetting errors, and time delays, yielding probability distributions for variant reaction rate k_variant. Thompson sampling balances exploration/exploitation: initially random, then increasingly selects promising variants. After Round 1, inactive variants dropped; after Round 2, TDoT-PT-BsGDH dominated (36 replicates in Round 3). Unseen variants became unlikely due to hierarchical model. TDoT-PT-BsGDH identified as best with high certainty.
process_signals: FlowerPlate limited to 48 strains; iterative screening needed.
Process model: first-order reaction, accounts for errors and delays.
Thompson sampling used for experimental design: unicates instead of triplicates.
Round 0: equal prior probabilities.
Round 1: inactive variants (e.g., (L6KD)n-SG-BsGDH) dropped to 0% probability.
Round 2: mixture of unseen and promising variants; TDoT-PT-BsGDH emerged.
Round 3: 36 replicates of TDoT-PT-BsGDH; convergence.
Hierarchical model reduced probability of unseen variants outperforming.
metrics: Replicates of top variant: 50 total across rounds
failures_or_limits: Inactive variants identified early and excluded.

section_gists.heading: Insights on structure/function relationship
section_gists.gist: Microscopy and SDS-PAGE of insoluble fractions confirmed CatIB formation. BsGDH_WT did not form CatIBs. Top Thompson sampling variants (TDoT-PT-BsGDH, BsGDH-PT-3HAMP, BsGDH-PT-CBDCell, BsGDH-PT-L6KD) all showed CatIBs by SDS-PAGE and microscopy. PT linker generally more active; C-terminal fusion often beneficial, but top performer TDoT-PT-BsGDH had N-terminal tag. Larger L6KD tags did not improve formation. No general rules; enzyme-specific screening necessary.
process_signals: BsGDH_WT no CatIBs, confirming previous study.
Top variants all had PT linker and detectable CatIBs.
C-terminal fusion generally better, but N-terminal top performer exception.
Larger L6KD tags did not enhance CatIB formation.
No generalizable rules; screening required per enzyme.
failures_or_limits: Limited understanding of structure-function relationship; a priori prediction still not possible.

section_gists.heading: Conclusions
section_gists.gist: Developed a CatIB toolbox enabling automated DBTL cycle: strain construction, cultivation, purification, characterization. Optimization eliminated positional bias, adapted assay, achieved 1.9% RSD. Manual work reduced by 88% for 48 variants. Thompson sampling identified TDoT-PT-BsGDH in 3 rounds with high certainty. PT linker again more successful, but prediction remains impossible. Future: feedback loop for strain construction, simultaneous optimization of variants and conditions, further automation for structure-function studies.
process_signals: Toolbox enables parallel analysis of CatIB candidates.
Optimization steps: temperature, liquid handling, online measurement.
Thompson sampling more efficient than triplicate design.
PT linker beneficial, but no a priori prediction.
Future: feedback loop, co-optimization, more automation.
failures_or_limits: A priori prediction still not possible.
Further automation and data needed for structure-function understanding.

section_gists.heading: Availability of data and materials
section_gists.gist: Screening data and Thompson sampling results available on GitHub. Other data available from corresponding author upon request.

global_hypotheses_about_storyline: The research evolved from the need to screen many CatIB variants efficiently because no a priori prediction of optimal linker-tag combinations exists.
Initial manual workflows were too laborious, motivating semi-automation of cloning and full automation of screening.
A critical technical challenge (positional bias in BioLector) was discovered during optimization, leading to a protocol change (constant 25°C) that resolved it and was validated.
The enzymatic assay was improved for online measurement, increasing data quality and reproducibility.

Bayesian optimization with Thompson sampling was integrated to efficiently explore the variant library within the limited capacity of the FlowerPlate, successfully identifying the best variant in fewer rounds than a traditional design.

The study confirmed that PT linker generally yields more active CatIBs, but exceptions (N-terminal top performer) highlight the need for empirical screening, reinforcing the value of the developed toolbox.
uncertainties_to_resolve: Why did 13 variants fail to clone? Were they truly non-viable, or could conditions be optimized?
How generalizable is the 25°C cultivation condition to other enzymes or CatIB systems?
Could the process model be improved to incorporate more biological factors (e.g., CatIB count, specific activity)?
What are the exact computational details of the Bayesian model and Thompson sampling implementation?
How would the workflow perform with even larger libraries or different enzymes?
What are the specific liquid handling settings and pre-warming parameters that were optimized?
Are there any limitations in the automated purification that could affect CatIB integrity or activity measurement?

events.summary: No a priori prediction possible for best linker/tag combination for CatIBs, necessitating extensive screening.
Semi-automated cloning workflow implemented for fast generation of 63 CatIB variants using Golden Gate Assembly.
13 CatIB variants could not be cloned within three attempts, potentially due to non-viable phenotype.
Systematic positional bias observed in BioLector backscatter values during cultivation at 15°C.
Hypothesis that temperature gradient from cooling fan caused positional bias.
Changed cultivation temperature to constant 25°C to eliminate positional bias.
Validation of automated screening workflow showed high reproducibility with 1.9% RSD across 42 biological replicates.

findings.problem: Step 3 incorrectly merges the observation of positional bias (K04) and the hypothesis about its cause (K05) into a single step, and its supporting_event_ids include K05 which is a hypothesis event, not an observation. The step should separate the observation and hypothesis, or at least not claim K05 as an observation.
findings.revision_instruction: Split step 3 into two steps: one for observing the bias (K04) and one for hypothesizing the cause (K05). Alternatively, adjust the observation field to reflect only the bias observation and move the hypothesis to the hypothesis field, ensuring supporting_event_ids only include K04.

findings.problem: Step 5 claims to validate both bias elimination (K07) and CatIB formation/activity (K08) in a single step, but the evidence provided for K07 (EV_C002_E04_02) only states bias prevention, and the evidence for K08 (EV_C002_E05_01) is a general statement about positive influence on formation rate, not a direct validation of activity for specific variants. The step also includes EV_C002_E01_02 which is about the bias before correction, not validation. The step should be split or evidence should be more precise.
findings.revision_instruction: Split step 5 into two validation steps: one for bias elimination (K07) with evidence EV_C002_E04_02, and one for CatIB formation/activity (K08) with evidence EV_C002_E05_01 and possibly EV_AUTO_012 or EV_AUTO_028. Remove EV_C002_E01_02 from this step.

findings.problem: Step 9 states '>95% probability' but the evidence EV_AUTO_010 says '98% probability'. The step should use the exact number from the evidence.
findings.revision_instruction: Change '>95% probability' to '98% probability' in step 9 observation, or note that the termination criterion was >95% and the actual probability was 98%.

findings.revision_instruction: Update state_before in step 10 to reflect the actual 98% probability from step 9.

findings.problem: Step 5 includes EV_C002_E01_02 which is about the bias before correction (mean 74.9, SD 4.5). This evidence is not directly supporting the validation of bias elimination; it belongs to the observation of bias in step 3.
findings.revision_instruction: Remove EV_C002_E01_02 from step 5 evidence list. It is already used in step 3.

evolution_trajectory.decision: Integrate Bayesian process model and Thompson sampling for experimental design.

tool_or_method.name: Bayesian process model and Thompson sampling

evolution_trajectory.observation: Combined automated workflow with Bayesian process model and Thompson sampling for iterative screening of 63 variants in three batch experiments of 48 candidates each.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Execute iterative screening and analyze results.

evidence.quote_or_span: the workflow was performed in combination with a Bayesian process model and Thompson sampling
allowed analysis of 63 BsGDH-CatIB variants within only three batch experiments
the process model was used to quantify various experimental effects and their influence on the reaction rate of each CatIB variant in the final activity assay
we chose to model their reaction in the activity assay with a first order mass action law
this results in an overall manual work time of 7 h for 48 variants. This is approximately 12% of the operator workload for the typical manual procedure

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Three rounds of iterative screening completed.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Which variant is the best performer?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Thompson sampling will identify the best variant with >95% probability.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Analyze results after three rounds; identify top candidate.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Thompson sampling identified TDoT-PT-BsGDH as best performer with 98% probability after three rounds; inactive variants dropped early. Top four candidates had median reaction rate constants between 0.15 min^-1 and 0.19 min^-1.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Validate top candidate with a manual fourth round.

evidence.quote_or_span: Due to the probabilistic nature of the algorithm, two remaining variants, BsGDH-SG-L48KD, and CBDCell-PT-BsGDH, where not chosen in any round.
the top four candidates, TDoT-PT-BsGDH, BsGDH-PT-3HAMP, BsGDH-PT-CBDCell and BsGDH-PT-L6KD all have a median reaction rate constant between 0.15 min $^{-1}$ and 0.19 min $^{-1}$
Afterwards, TDoT-PT-BsGDH showed a 98% probability to be the best performer in the library (see Round 3), which exceed the termination criterion of $>95\%$ probability.

Due to the exploitation-exploration balancing, the Thompson sampling algorithm selected this variant in 50 biological replicates during the three screening rounds, leading to a statistically sound estimation of its reaction rate constant as the KPI for a library ranking.

evidence.quote_or_span: the top performer TDoT-PT-BsGDH is a variant with N-terminal addition of the tag, as such a perfect example of the still limited understanding of the structure–function relationship for CatIB design.
successful CatIB formation and activity highly depend on the combination of aggregation-inducing tag, length and type of the linker and N-/C-terminal fusion, allowing for no general conclusion

success_verification.final_conclusion: TDoT-PT-BsGDH identified as best CatIB variant with >99% probability; automated workflow reduced manual work by 88% and achieved 1.9% RSD.
limitations: Limited understanding of structure-function relationship for CatIB design; no general design rules can be derived.
13 variants could not be cloned, potentially due to non-viable phenotypes.
